questions.question: The "root" of the thoracic aorta is defined as the portion extending between which two landmarks?
choices: Aortic valve annulus and the left subclavian artery
Aortic valve annulus and the sino-tubular (ST) junction
ST junction and the innominate artery
Left subclavian artery and the ligamentum arteriosum
questions.detail: The aortic root specifically extends from the valve annulus to the sino-tubular junction.

questions.question: What is the average diameter of the thoracic aorta at the root?
questions.detail: The thoracic aorta averages 3.6 cm at the root, tapering to 2.4 cm distally.

questions.question: Which structure is located just distal to the aortic isthmus and is considered a normal contour bulge rather than a pseudoaneurysm?
questions.detail: A ductus bump is a normal contour bulge found along the lesser curvature distal to the isthmus.

questions.question: The "Bovine Arch" variant, seen in 15% of the population, is defined by which anatomical arrangement?
choices: Separate origin of the left vertebral artery
Common origin of the brachiocephalic and left common carotid arteries
Left common carotid coming off the brachiocephalic proper
Four separate branches off the arch
Right-sided aortic arch with mirror branching
questions.detail: Bovine arch is characterized by a shared origin of the brachiocephalic and left common carotid arteries.

questions.question: What is the most common aortic arch anomaly?
choices: Left arch with an aberrant right subclavian artery
Separate origin of the left vertebral artery
questions.detail: The left arch with an aberrant right subclavian is the most frequent arch anomaly.

questions.question: Which vascular anomaly is unique as the only one to course between the esophagus and the trachea?
questions.detail: The pulmonary sling is the only variant that passes between the esophagus and the trachea.

questions.question: Pulmonary sling is characteristically associated with which of the following?
questions.detail: Pulmonary slings are highly associated with primary tracheal stenosis due to complete tracheal rings.

questions.question: The Artery of Adamkiewicz, a dominant feeder of the spinal cord, most commonly arises from which side of the aorta?
questions.detail: The Artery of Adamkiewicz arises from the left side in 70% of cases.

questions.question: What is the most common vertebral level range for the origin of the Artery of Adamkiewicz?
questions.detail: This critical spinal artery typically originates between T9 and T12.

questions.question: On an angiogram, the Artery of Adamkiewicz is identified by which characteristic morphology?
questions.detail: The classic 'hairpin turn' identifies this artery where it joins the anterior spinal artery.

questions.question: Which branch is typically the first to arise from the Superior Mesenteric Artery (SMA)?
questions.detail: The inferior pancreaticoduodenal is the first branch to take off from the SMA.

questions.question: The "Arc of Riolan" is a collateral pathway connecting which two vessels?
choices: Right colic and middle colic arteries
Left colic (from IMA) and middle colic (from SMA)
questions.detail: The Arc of Riolan connects the left colic (IMA) and middle colic (SMA) arteries.

questions.question: A "Typical" Celiac Axis gives rise to which three branches?
choices: Left gastric, SMA, and splenic arteries
Common hepatic, left gastric, and splenic arteries
Proper hepatic, GDA, and splenic arteries
Right hepatic, left hepatic, and GDA
Common hepatic, right gastric, and IMA
questions.detail: The standard celiac trifurcation includes the common hepatic, left gastric, and splenic arteries.

questions.question: What is the most common variation of the hepatic arterial anatomy (seen in ~10% of people)?
choices: Replaced left hepatic artery off the left gastric
Replaced right hepatic artery off the SMA
Replaced common hepatic artery off the SMA
questions.detail: A replaced right hepatic artery from the SMA is the most common variant (10%).

questions.question: If a vessel is seen in the fissure of the ligamentum venosum on CT, what does it likely represent?
choices: Replaced or accessory left hepatic artery
questions.detail: A replaced or accessory left hepatic artery from the left gastric artery courses through the fissure of the ligamentum venosum.

questions.question: Which mnemonic is used to remember the branches of the posterior division of the internal iliac artery?
questions.detail: The mnemonic stands for Iliolumbar, Lateral sacral, and Superior gluteal arteries.

questions.question: The "Persistent Sciatic Artery" is an anatomical variant that is a continuation of which vessel?
questions.detail: This artery is a continuation of the internal iliac artery.

questions.question: What is a classic clinical clue for a persistent sciatic artery on a vascular boards question?
choices: Absent femoral pulses but a strong pulse in the foot
Tearing chest pain radiating to the back
questions.detail: This occurs because the foot is supplied by the internal iliac system rather than the occluded external/femoral system.

questions.question: "Corona Mortis" (Crown of Death) is a vascular connection between which two arteries?
choices: Internal iliac and internal pudendal arteries
questions.detail: Corona Mortis is an anastomosis between the obturator and external iliac (or inferior epigastric) systems.

questions.question: Why is the "Corona Mortis" significant in clinical practice?
choices: It is a common source of type 1 endoleaks.
it can be injured during pelvic trauma or surgery and is difficult to ligate.
It is the main feeder for the spinal cord.
it prevents the development of collateral circulation.
questions.detail: Its location makes it prone to surgical injury and notoriously difficult to control.

questions.question: In the scalene triangle of the neck, what is the relationship between the subclavian artery and vein?
choices: The artery is anterior to the vein.
The artery is posterior to the vein.
Both run inside the triangle with the phrenic nerve.
The vein runs in the triangle; the artery runs anteriorly.
They are separated by the middle scalene muscle.
questions.detail: The subclavian artery lies posterior to the vein in the scalene triangle.

questions.question: At which landmark does the axillary artery become the brachial artery?
choices: The lower border of the teres major
The level of the radial head
The lower border of the teres minor
The surgical neck of the humerus
questions.detail: The transition from axillary to brachial occurs at the lower border of the teres major muscle.

questions.question: Which of the following is a rule for distinguishing the ulnar artery from the radial artery on an angiogram?
choices: The radial artery is usually bigger.
The ulnar artery supplies the deep palmar arch.
The ulnar artery usually gives off the common interosseous artery.
The radial artery is always medial.
The ulnar artery originates from the axillary artery.
questions.detail: The ulnar artery is typically larger and provides the common interosseous branch.

questions.question: At the inguinal ligament, the external iliac artery becomes which vessel?
questions.detail: The external iliac becomes the common femoral artery after passing the inguinal ligament.

questions.question: Which artery is considered the most medial artery in the lower leg?
questions.detail: The posterior tibialis is the most medial and is palpated at the medial malleolus.

questions.question: The popliteal artery bifurcates into which two primary vessels?
choices: Anterior tibialis and the tibioperoneal trunk
questions.detail: At the lower border of the popliteus, the artery splits into the anterior tibialis and the tibioperoneal trunk.

questions.question: Most gastric varices are formed by which vessel?
questions.detail: The left gastric (coronary) vein is the primary source of gastric varices.

questions.question: What is the most common congenital venous anomaly in the chest?
questions.detail: Left-sided SVC is the most frequent congenital venous chest anomaly.

questions.question: A persistent left-sided SVC nearly always (92%) drains into which structure?
questions.detail: In the vast majority of cases, the left SVC drains into the coronary sinus.

questions.question: Left-sided SVC is characteristically associated with which congenital heart defect?
questions.detail: Atrial septal defects (ASD) are most commonly associated with a left-sided SVC.

questions.question: "Azygos continuation" of the IVC is also known as the absence of which segment?
questions.detail: Azygos continuation is defined by the absence of the hepatic IVC segment.

questions.question: The presence of azygos continuation should immediately make you think of which syndrome?
questions.detail: Azygos continuation of the IVC is a hallmark of polysplenia syndrome.

questions.question: In the "Heterotaxia Syndrome" game, which finding is associated with asplenia (right isomerism)?
choices: One fissure in the right lung
questions.detail: Asplenia or Right Isomerism is associated with a reversed relationship between the Aorta and IVC.

questions.question: In a "circumaortic venous collar," which limb is superior relative to the other?
choices: They are at the same level.
It depends on the patient's age.
The superior limb is always the one that thromboses.
questions.detail: The anterior limb of the collar is superior, while the posterior limb is inferior.

questions.question: A "Duplicated IVC" is frequently associated with which anatomical anomalies?
choices: Horseshoe kidney and crossed fused ectopic kidneys
questions.detail: Duplicated IVCs are commonly associated with horseshoe or crossed fused renal ectopia.

questions.question: Where is the most common location for a pulmonary AVM?
questions.detail: Due to higher blood flow, pulmonary AVMs are most frequently found in the lower lobes.

questions.question: At what afferent vessel size is a pulmonary AVM typically treated?
questions.detail: The clinical standard is to treat AVMs when the feeding artery reaches 3 mm.

questions.question: Which syndrome is characterized by multiple AVMs in the liver and lungs?
questions.detail: Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia (HHT) typically presents with multiple visceral AVMs.

questions.question: In a patient with HHT, what is a feared complication of pulmonary AVMs due to right-to-left shunting?
questions.detail: Right-to-left shunting through pulmonary AVMs can allow bacteria or clots to reach the brain, causing abscess or stroke.

questions.question: What is the most common visceral arterial aneurysm?
questions.detail: The splenic artery is the most frequent site of visceral arterial aneurysms.

questions.question: Splenic artery aneurysms are more common and more likely to rupture in which patient population?
questions.detail: Pregnancy is a major risk factor for the development and rupture of splenic aneurysms.

questions.question: For visceral artery aneurysms, what is the generally accepted size threshold for treatment?
questions.detail: Most visceral aneurysms (splenic, hepatic) are treated once they reach 2 cm.

questions.question: Which visceral aneurysm carries a high rate of rupture and mesenteric ischemia and should always be treated?
questions.detail: All SMA aneurysms require treatment due to their aggressive nature and risk of ischemia.

questions.question: "Median Arcuate Ligament Syndrome" (Dunbar Syndrome) is caused by compression of which vessel?
questions.detail: This syndrome involves celiac artery compression by the median arcuate ligament.

questions.question: What is the characteristic radiographic buzzword for Median Arcuate Ligament Syndrome?
questions.detail: The 'hooked appearance' of the celiac artery is diagnostic for this syndrome.

questions.question: Symptoms of Median Arcuate Ligament Syndrome characteristically get worse during which phase of respiration?
questions.detail: Compression of the celiac artery is classically worsened during expiration.

questions.question: Which collateral system often dilates in the setting of celiac artery stenosis/occlusion?
questions.detail: Stenosis of the celiac axis often leads to compensatory dilation of the pancreaticoduodenal collateral arcades.

questions.question: In the "Anatomy of the Secondary Pulmonary Lobule," what structure runs through the middle?
questions.detail: The terminal bronchiole and pulmonary artery occupy the center of the lobule.

questions.question: What structures are found in the periphery of the secondary pulmonary lobule, outlining the septa?
questions.detail: The veins and lymphatics run in the interlobular septa at the periphery.

questions.question: What is the "most anterior" structure in the superior mediastinum?
questions.detail: The thymus is the most anteriorly situated structure in the superior mediastinum.

questions.question: What are the three recognized "acute aortic syndromes"?
choices: Dissection, intramural hematoma, and penetrating ulcer
questions.detail: Aortic dissection, intramural hematoma, and penetrating ulcer comprise the AAS triad.

questions.question: What is the #1 risk factor for the development of a Penetrating Ulcer?
questions.detail: Atherosclerosis is the primary risk factor for a penetrating aortic ulcer.

questions.question: Penetrating ulcers typically involve which portion of the aorta?
questions.detail: These ulcers most frequently affect the descending thoracic aorta in elderly patients.

questions.question: Which imaging "pearl" confirms that an ulcer has truly penetrated through the intima?
choices: A gap in the intimal calcifications
Reflux of contrast into the azygos vein
questions.detail: A break or gap in intimal calcifications indicates the ulcer has breached the intimal layer.

questions.question: Why do penetrating ulcers almost never occur in the aortic root?
choices: High flow pressures prevent cheeseburger crumbs (atherosclerosis) from depositing.
The root lacks an intimal layer.
The root is protected by the pericardium.
questions.detail: The high-velocity blood flow in the root prevents the deposition of atherosclerosis.

questions.question: What is the most common cause (70%) of an acute aortic syndrome?
questions.detail: Aortic dissection is responsible for 70% of acute aortic syndrome cases.

questions.question: What is the main factor leading to an intimal tear in an aortic dissection?
questions.detail: Hypertension is the dominant driver leading to the initial intimal tear.

questions.question: Stanford A dissections involve the ascending aorta and must be treated:
questions.detail: Stanford A dissections are surgical emergencies due to the risk of root involvement.

questions.question: Stanford B dissections occur distal to the takeoff of the left subclavian and are typically treated:
questions.detail: Unless complicated by ischemia, Stanford B dissections are managed with medical therapy.

questions.question: On CT, which feature helps identify the "False Lumen" in an aortic dissection?
choices: Continuity with the undissected portion of the aorta
questions.detail: The 'Cobweb Sign' (linear low-attenuation areas) is a hallmark of the false lumen.

questions.question: The "Beak Sign" (an acute angle at the edge of the lumen) is a characteristic of which lumen?
choices: It is only seen in penetrating ulcers
It is only seen in Stanford B dissections
questions.detail: The 'Beak Sign' is an acute angle characteristic of the false lumen.

questions.question: In a dissection, which renal artery usually arises from the "False Lumen"?
questions.detail: The false lumen typically contains the origin of the left renal artery.

questions.question: Which "game" is used to remember that the false lumen is associated with the left side and "evil"?
questions.detail: The mnemonic 'False is Left' helps associate the false lumen with the left renal artery.

questions.question: The "Floating Viscera Sign" is a classic angiographic sign of what?
questions.detail: This angiographic sign occurs when branch vessels appear to arise from nowhere in a dissection.

questions.question: A dissection flap in the abdomen that is "Static" is located in the feeding artery and is usually treated with:
questions.detail: Static flaps within feeding vessels are primarily treated with stents.

questions.question: A "Dynamic" dissection flap in the abdomen dangles in front of the ostium and is usually treated with:
questions.detail: Dynamic flaps obstructing vessel ostia are usually treated via fenestration.

questions.question: How can you distinguish a thrombosed dissection from an aneurysm with mural thrombus?
choices: The dissection flap should spiral; the thrombus tends to drop straight down.
Aneurysms are only seen in the arch.
questions.detail: Dissections typically spiral and displace intimal calcifications, unlike simple mural thrombus.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of an Intramural Hematoma (IMH)?
questions.detail: Hypertension is the most common etiology for an intramural hematoma.

questions.question: How does an Intramural Hematoma appear on non-contrast CT?
questions.detail: IMH presents as a hyperdense crescentic area on non-contrast CT.

questions.question: Which of the following is a predictor of a "shitty outcome" for an Intramural Hematoma?
choices: Association with an aortic diameter of 3 cm
questions.detail: A hematoma thicker than 2 cm is a poor prognostic indicator for IMH.

questions.question: A true aneurysm is defined as an enlargement of the vessel lumen to at least how many times its normal diameter?
questions.detail: By definition, a true aneurysm must be at least 1.5 times the normal vessel diameter.

questions.question: What is the fundamental difference between a true aneurysm and a pseudoaneurysm?
choices: True aneurysms are always bigger than 5 cm.
In a pseudoaneurysm, all 3 layers are NOT intact.
True aneurysms are only caused by trauma.
questions.detail: In a pseudoaneurysm, the vessel wall layers are compromised, making it a contained rupture.

questions.question: In a living patient, where is the most common site for a "Traumatic Pseudoaneurysm"?
questions.detail: 90% of traumatic pseudoaneurysms seen in living patients occur at the aortic isthmus.

questions.question: Why is the aortic isthmus the most common site for traumatic injury?
choices: It is the thinnest part of the aorta.
It is the result of tethering from the ligamentum arteriosum.
It has the highest flow pressures.
It is adjacent to the esophagus.
questions.detail: The tethering effect of the ligamentum arteriosum makes the isthmus vulnerable to deceleration injury.

questions.question: What is the #1 cause of SVC Syndrome?
choices: External compression from lymphoma or lung cancer
questions.detail: External compression from malignancy is the primary cause of SVC syndrome.

questions.question: What are the two primary causes of ascending aortic calcifications, given that atherosclerosis typically spares this region?
questions.detail: Takayasu vasculitis and syphilis are the classic causes of ascending aortic calcification.

questions.question: What is the general rule for surgical repair of an aortic aneurysm in the chest?
questions.detail: Chest aneurysms are generally repaired when they reach 6 cm in diameter.

questions.question: Sinus of Valsalva aneurysms are most common in which demographic and typically involve which sinus?
choices: Young African American men; Non-coronary sinus
questions.detail: These aneurysms are most common in Asian men and typically involve the right sinus.

questions.question: What is the most common associated cardiac anomaly with a Sinus of Valsalva aneurysm?
questions.detail: Ventricular septal defect (VSD) is the most frequent associated anomaly.

questions.question: Which of the following is a specific imaging sign of an "Impending Rupture" of an aortic aneurysm?
questions.detail: The 'Draped Aorta Sign' indicates that the posterior wall of the aorta is failing and draping over the spine.

questions.question: The "Hyperdense Crescent Sign" on non-contrast CT represents what in the context of an aneurysm?
choices: Hemorrhage and a manifestation of impending rupture
questions.detail: The hyperdense crescent sign indicates acute intramural hemorrhage and impending rupture.

questions.question: Mycotic aneurysms are typically described by which morphology and etiology?
questions.detail: Mycotic aneurysms are saccular pseudoaneurysms resulting from hematogenous seeding during sepsis.

questions.question: If you see a saccular aneurysm of the abdominal aorta (especially one that is small and expanding fast), what must you consider?
questions.detail: A small, rapidly expanding saccular abdominal aneurysm should be treated as infected until proven otherwise.

questions.question: Vascular findings in Neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF-1) include aneurysms and which common presentation in children?
choices: Orificial renal artery stenosis with hypertension
questions.detail: The classic vascular manifestation of NF-1 in teenagers is orificial renal artery stenosis.

questions.question: Dilation of the aortic root in Marfan Syndrome is classically described as what?
questions.detail: Annuloaortic ectasia with a 'tulip bulb' appearance is the classic Marfan root finding.

questions.question: What is a common and serious complication of the aortic root dilation in Marfan syndrome?
questions.detail: Root dilation leads to stretching of the valve ring and subsequent aortic insufficiency.

questions.question: Loeys Dietz Syndrome is described as a "really shitty version of Marfans" and is associated with which unique feature?
questions.detail: Bifid uvula or cleft palate is part of the characteristic clinical triad for Loeys Dietz.

questions.question: Patients with Loeys Dietz Syndrome have vessels that are characteristically:
questions.detail: Extreme vessel tortuosity is a hallmark of Loeys Dietz syndrome.

questions.question: In Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, which procedure should be avoided due to the excessive risk of arterial dissection?
questions.detail: The extreme arterial fragility in Ehlers-Danlos makes invasive angiography dangerous.

questions.question: Which rare aneurysm is associated with "tree bark" intimal calcifications and involves the ascending aorta and arch branches?
questions.detail: Syphilitic aneurysms typically involve the ascending aorta and feature 'tree bark' calcifications.

questions.question: Secondary aortoenteric fistulas are much more common than primary ones and are seen after:
choices: Aortic surgery with or without stent graft placement
questions.detail: Most aortoenteric fistulas occur as a complication following aortic surgical repair.

questions.question: What part of the bowel is most commonly involved in an aortoenteric fistula?
questions.detail: The 3rd and 4th portions of the duodenum are the most frequent sites of fistula formation.

questions.question: What is the definitive imaging finding for an aortoenteric fistula?
choices: Contrast from the aorta into the bowel lumen
questions.detail: Direct visualization of contrast flowing from the aorta into the bowel is the only definitive sign.

questions.question: "Inflammatory Aneurysms" are characteristically found in young men who smoke and characteristically spare which part of the aorta?
questions.detail: Inflammatory aneurysms typically involve asymmetrical thickening that spares the posterior wall.

questions.question: The clinical triad of "Leriche Syndrome" includes which three findings?
choices: Impotence (limp dick), ass claudication, and absent femoral pulses
Hypertelorism, bifid uvula, and tortuous vessels
Pectus excavatum, ectopic lens, and tulip bulb aorta
questions.detail: Leriche syndrome is defined by impotence, buttock claudication, and absent femoral pulses.

questions.question: What is the "Aunt Minnie" sign for Coarctation of the Aorta on a chest X-ray?
questions.detail: The 'Figure 3 sign' is the classic CXR appearance of aortic coarctation.

questions.question: Rib notching in coarctation characteristically spares which ribs?
choices: All ribs on the left side
questions.detail: The 1st and 2nd ribs are spared because they are supplied by the costocervical trunk.

questions.question: "Pseudocoarctation" looks like a coarctation but is distinguished by the absence of what?
choices: A pressure gradient, collateral formation, and rib notching
questions.detail: Pseudocoarctation lacks the pressure gradient and collateral hallmarks of true coarctation.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of Thoracic Outlet Syndrome?
choices: Compression by the anterior scalene muscle
questions.detail: Anterior scalene muscle compression is the leading cause of thoracic outlet syndrome.

questions.question: "Paget Schroetter" syndrome, also known as "effort thrombosis," involves a venous thrombus in which vessel?
questions.detail: Paget Schroetter refers to subclavian vein thrombosis related to thoracic outlet compression.
